news_summary: Recent health breakthroughs reveal a sleep-related mechanism enhancing muscle, fat loss, and brainpower; an effective cholesterol drug significantly reducing heart attack risk; superior non-drug therapies for knee arthritis pain; and the first detailed mapping of clitoral nerves, advancing understanding of female anatomy.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes dans le domaine de la santé mettent en lumière un mécanisme lié au sommeil qui favorise le développement musculaire, la perte de graisse et les capacités cognitives ; un médicament efficace contre le cholestérol qui réduit considérablement le risque de crise cardiaque ; des traitements non médicamenteux particulièrement efficaces contre les douleurs liées à l'arthrose du genou ; ainsi que la première cartographie détaillée des nerfs clitoridiens, qui permet de mieux comprendre l'anatomie féminine.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud revelan un mecanismo relacionado con el sueño que mejora la masa muscular, la pérdida de grasa y la capacidad intelectual; un fármaco eficaz contra el colesterol que reduce significativamente el riesgo de sufrir un infarto; terapias no farmacológicas de gran eficacia para el dolor de la artritis de rodilla; y el primer mapa detallado de los nervios del clítoris, que permite avanzar en la comprensión de la anatomía femenina.

articles.title: Scientists discover sleep switch that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts brainpower
articles.summary: Deep sleep does far more than rest the body — it activates a powerful brain-driven system that controls growth hormone, fueling muscle and bone strength, metabolism, and even mental performance. Scientists have now mapped the neural circuits behind this process, uncovering a delicate feedback loop in which sleep boosts growth hormone, and that same hormone helps regulate wakefulness.
articles.content:
Deep sleep does more than help you feel rested. It actively rebuilds your body, strengthening muscles, supporting bone growth, and helping burn fat. For teenagers, it is also essential for reaching full height potential.

At the center of all this is growth hormone, which surges during sleep. But scientists have long puzzled over why poor sleep, especially the early deep stage known as non-REM sleep, leads to lower levels of this critical hormone.

Scientists Discover the Brain Circuit Behind It

Researchers at the University of California, Berkeley, have now uncovered the answer. In a study published in Cell, they mapped the brain circuits that control growth hormone release during sleep and identified a new feedback system that keeps those levels in balance.

This discovery offers a clearer understanding of how sleep and hormones work together. It may also open the door to new treatments for sleep disorders linked to metabolic diseases like diabetes, as well as neurological conditions such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's.

"People know that growth hormone release is tightly related to sleep, but only through drawing blood and checking growth hormone levels during sleep," said study first author Xinlu Ding, a postdoctoral fellow in UC Berkeley's Department of Neuroscience and the Helen Wills Neuroscience Institute. "We're actually directly recording neural activity in mice to see what's going on. We are providing a basic circuit to work on in the future to develop different treatments."

Lack of sleep does more than leave you tired. Because growth hormone helps control how the body processes sugar and fat, poor sleep can increase the risk of obesity, diabetes, and heart disease.

The Brain Regions Driving Growth Hormone

The system behind this process is buried deep in the hypothalamus, an ancient part of the brain shared by all mammals. Here, specialized neurons release signals that either trigger or suppress growth hormone.

Two key players are growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH), which stimulates release, and somatostatin, which inhibits it. Together, they coordinate hormone activity across the sleep-wake cycle.

Once growth hormone enters the system, it activates the locus coeruleus, a brainstem region that controls alertness, attention, and cognitive function. Disruptions in this area are linked to a wide range of neurological and psychiatric disorders.

"Understanding the neural circuit for growth hormone release could eventually point toward new hormonal therapies to improve sleep quality or restore normal growth hormone balance," said Daniel Silverman, a UC Berkeley postdoctoral fellow and study co-author. "There are some experimental gene therapies where you target a specific cell type. This circuit could be a novel handle to try to dial back the excitability of the locus coeruleus, which hasn't been talked about before."

How Sleep Stages Control Hormone Release

To study this system, researchers recorded brain activity in mice by inserting electrodes and stimulating neurons with light. Because mice sleep in short bursts throughout the day and night, they provided a detailed view of how growth hormone changes across sleep stages.

The team found that GHRH and somatostatin behave differently depending on whether the brain is in REM or non-REM sleep.

During REM sleep, both hormones increase, leading to a surge in growth hormone. During non-REM sleep, somatostatin drops while GHRH rises more modestly, still boosting hormone levels but in a different pattern.

A Surprising Feedback Loop in the Brain

The researchers also uncovered a feedback loop that links growth hormone to wakefulness. As sleep continues, growth hormone gradually builds up and stimulates the locus coeruleus, nudging the brain toward waking.

But there is a twist. When this brain region becomes too active, it can actually trigger sleepiness instead, creating a delicate balance between sleep and alertness.

"This suggests that sleep and growth hormone form a tightly balanced system: Too little sleep reduces growth hormone release, and too much growth hormone can in turn push the brain toward wakefulness," Silverman said. "Sleep drives growth hormone release, and growth hormone feeds back to regulate wakefulness, and this balance is essential for growth, repair and metabolic health."

Why It Matters for Brain and Body

This balance does more than affect physical growth. Because growth hormone works through brain systems that control alertness, it may also influence how clearly you think and how focused you feel.

"Growth hormone not only helps you build your muscle and bones and reduce your fat tissue, but may also have cognitive benefits, promoting your overall arousal level when you wake up," Ding said.

Funding and Research Team

The research was supported by the Howard Hughes Medical Institute (HHMI) and the Pivotal Life Sciences Chancellor's Chair fund. Yang Dan holds the Pivotal Life Sciences Chancellor's Chair in Neuroscience. The study also included collaborators from UC Berkeley and Stanford University.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery mapping the brain circuits controlling growth hormone release during sleep, which has broad implications for improving sleep quality, metabolic health, and cognitive function. This breakthrough offers potential new treatments for sleep disorders and metabolic and neurological diseases, benefiting a wide population. The article is focused, detailed, and explains the discovery's mechanisms and impact clearly.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify brain circuit that links deep sleep to muscle growth and fat loss
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Deep sleep is known to help the body rest, but it also plays a key role in building muscles, strengthening bones, burning fat, and supporting brain function. Growth hormone, which increases during sleep, is essential for these benefits. However, scientists did not fully understand how sleep controls the release of this hormone.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California, Berkeley, discovered the brain circuits that regulate growth hormone during sleep. By studying mice, they found how two brain chemicals, growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) and somatostatin, work together during different sleep stages to control hormone release. They also uncovered a feedback loop where growth hormone influences brain areas that manage wakefulness.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why poor sleep lowers growth hormone levels and why this can increase risks for obesity, diabetes, and heart disease. It also shows how growth hormone affects attention and alertness. Understanding this brain system opens new possibilities for treating sleep problems and related diseases like Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use this knowledge to develop new treatments that improve sleep quality and balance growth hormone levels. Future work may explore gene therapies targeting specific brain cells to help people with sleep or metabolic disorders.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have mapped how deep sleep controls growth hormone release through brain circuits, revealing new ways to improve health and sleep-related diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques identifient le circuit cérébral qui relie le sommeil profond à la croissance musculaire et à la perte de graisse
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le sommeil profond ne sert pas seulement à se reposer, il joue aussi un rôle crucial dans la construction des muscles, le renforcement des os, la combustion des graisses et le soutien des fonctions cérébrales. L’hormone de croissance, qui augmente pendant le sommeil, est essentielle pour ces bienfaits. Pourtant, les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas entièrement comment le sommeil contrôle la libération de cette hormone.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à Berkeley ont découvert les circuits cérébraux qui régulent l’hormone de croissance pendant le sommeil. En étudiant des souris, ils ont observé comment deux substances chimiques du cerveau, l’hormone de libération de l’hormone de croissance (GHRH) et la somatostatine, travaillent ensemble pendant différentes phases du sommeil pour contrôler la libération de l’hormone. Ils ont aussi découvert une boucle de rétroaction où l’hormone de croissance influence les zones du cerveau qui gèrent l’éveil.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi un mauvais sommeil réduit les niveaux d’hormone de croissance et augmente les risques d’obésité, de diabète et de maladies cardiaques. Elle montre aussi comment cette hormone affecte l’attention et la vigilance. Comprendre ce système cérébral ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour traiter les troubles du sommeil et des maladies comme Parkinson et Alzheimer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser ces connaissances pour développer de nouveaux traitements qui améliorent la qualité du sommeil et l’équilibre des hormones de croissance. Des recherches futures pourraient explorer des thérapies géniques ciblant des cellules cérébrales spécifiques pour aider les personnes souffrant de troubles du sommeil ou métaboliques.

**Phrase-clé** – Les scientifiques ont cartographié comment le sommeil profond contrôle la libération de l’hormone de croissance via des circuits cérébraux, révélant de nouvelles voies pour améliorer la santé et les maladies liées au sommeil.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican el circuito cerebral que conecta el sueño profundo con el crecimiento muscular y la quema de grasa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Se sabe que el sueño profundo ayuda al cuerpo a descansar, pero también juega un papel clave en la construcción de músculos, el fortalecimiento de huesos, la quema de grasa y el apoyo a la función cerebral. La hormona del crecimiento, que aumenta durante el sueño, es esencial para estos beneficios. Sin embargo, los científicos no entendían completamente cómo el sueño controla la liberación de esta hormona.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California, Berkeley, descubrieron los circuitos cerebrales que regulan la hormona del crecimiento durante el sueño. Estudiando ratones, encontraron cómo dos químicos cerebrales, la hormona liberadora de la hormona del crecimiento (GHRH) y la somatostatina, trabajan juntos durante diferentes etapas del sueño para controlar la liberación hormonal. También descubrieron un bucle de retroalimentación donde la hormona del crecimiento afecta áreas cerebrales que controlan la vigilia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica por qué el mal sueño reduce los niveles de hormona del crecimiento y aumenta el riesgo de obesidad, diabetes y enfermedades cardíacas. También muestra cómo la hormona afecta la atención y el estado de alerta. Entender este sistema cerebral abre nuevas posibilidades para tratar problemas de sueño y enfermedades relacionadas como Parkinson y Alzheimer.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar este conocimiento para desarrollar nuevos tratamientos que mejoren la calidad del sueño y el equilibrio de la hormona del crecimiento. Investigaciones futuras podrían explorar terapias génicas dirigidas a células cerebrales específicas para ayudar a personas con trastornos del sueño o metabólicos.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos han mapeado cómo el sueño profundo controla la liberación de la hormona del crecimiento a través de circuitos cerebrales, revelando nuevas formas de mejorar la salud y las enfermedades relacionadas con el sueño.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene night scene inside a stylized brain-shaped landscape where glowing neural pathways gently pulse with light, showing two distinct clusters of softly illuminated orbs representing growth hormone releasing and inhibiting neurons communicating in harmony, while a subtle radiant stream flows from this core toward a calm, glowing locus coeruleus area symbolized by a tranquil blue beacon, all rendered in natural, muted tones to evoke balance, renewal, and the restorative power of deep sleep.

articles.title: Powerful cholesterol drug cuts heart attack risk by 31%
articles.summary: A powerful cholesterol-lowering drug may be changing the rules of heart disease prevention. Researchers found that evolocumab, typically used for people who already have cardiovascular disease, can significantly cut the risk of first-time heart attacks and strokes in high-risk patients with diabetes—even before any artery-clogging plaque is detected.
articles.content:
Mass General Brigham researchers report that the cholesterol-lowering drug evolocumab can significantly reduce the risk of a first major cardiovascular event in people with diabetes who are considered high risk but do not yet have diagnosed atherosclerosis (the build-up of plaque inside artery walls). The findings were presented at the American College of Cardiology's Annual Scientific Session & Expo and published at the same time in JAMA.

"For over a decade, the intensive cholesterol-lowering have been reserved for patients who already have cardiovascular disease," said corresponding author Nicholas A. Marston, MD, MPH, a cardiologist with the Mass General Brigham Heart and Vascular Institute. "These results demonstrate the benefit of intensive lowering cholesterol earlier and should change how we think about the prevention of heart attacks, strokes, and heart disease in patients without known significant atherosclerosis."

Why Lowering "Bad Cholesterol" Matters

Heart disease continues to be the leading cause of death worldwide. Reducing levels of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), often called "bad cholesterol," is one of the most effective ways to lower risk. Evolocumab belongs to a class of drugs known as PCSK9 inhibitors and can cut LDL-C levels by about 60%. It is typically used alongside statins, which are the standard treatment. However, people without atherosclerosis but at high risk are usually treated only with statins, if they receive medication at all.

The results come from a subgroup analysis of the VESALIUS-CV randomized trial, funded by Amgen Inc. Researchers studied 3,655 patients who had high-risk diabetes but no significant atherosclerosis. High-risk diabetes included people who had the condition for at least 10 years, required daily insulin, or had diabetes-related small blood vessel damage.

Participants were assigned to receive either evolocumab injections every two weeks or a placebo. All participants continued standard cholesterol treatments such as statins and ezetimibe during the study.

Significant Drop in Cholesterol Levels

Patients treated with evolocumab experienced much larger reductions in cholesterol. After 48 weeks, median LDL-C levels were about 51% lower in the evolocumab group compared with the placebo group (52 mg/dL versus 111mg/dL).

Lower Risk of First Heart Attack or Stroke

Over a follow-up period of nearly five years, those receiving evolocumab in addition to standard therapy had a 31% lower risk of experiencing their first major cardiovascular event. These events included death from coronary heart disease, heart attack, or ischemic stroke.

At five years, 5% of patients in the evolocumab group had experienced an event, compared with 7.1% in the placebo group.

Safety and Future Research

Serious side effects were reported at similar rates in both groups, indicating that the treatment was generally well tolerated.

Researchers note that additional studies will be needed to determine whether these benefits apply to other high-risk groups who do not yet have established atherosclerosis.

Authors, Disclosures, and Funding

In addition to Marston, Mass General Brigham contributors include Erin A. Bohula, Jeong-Gun Park, Sabina A. Murphy, Ron Blankstein, Robert P. Giugliano, and Marc S. Sabatine. Other authors include Ajay K. Bhatia, Gaetano M. De Ferrari, Lawrence A. Leiter, Jose C. Nicolau, Emileigh Walsh, Lyrica Liu, Subodh Verma, Naveed Sattar, Stephen J. Nicholls, Jose Lopez-Sendon, Ioanna Gouni-Berthold, Lale Tokgozoglu, Marcoli Cyrille, and Gabriel Paiva da Silva Lima.

Disclosures: Marston, Bohula, Kuder, Park, Murphy, Giugliano, and Sabatine are members of the TIMI Study Group. The TIMI Study Group reports grant support through Brigham and Women's Hospital from Amgen and other pharmaceutical companies. Marston, Bohula, De Ferrari, Nicolau, Gouni-Berthold Tokgozoglu, Giugliano, and Sabatine report personal fees from Amgen. Bhatia, Walsh, Liu, Cyrille, and Paiva da Silva Lima are employees and stockholders of Amgen. Blankstein reports research support and consulting fees from Amgen Inc. Giugliano reports honoraria for lectures and CME programs from Amgen. Additional author disclosures can be found in the paper.

Funding: Amgen Inc.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where the cholesterol-lowering drug evolocumab reduces the risk of first major cardiovascular events by 31% in high-risk diabetic patients without diagnosed atherosclerosis. This has broad public health implications given heart disease is the leading cause of death worldwide, and the study provides detailed clinical evidence and context supporting a potential change in preventive treatment guidelines.
articles.personality_title: New cholesterol drug lowers first heart attack risk by 31% in high-risk diabetes patients
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heart disease is the leading cause of death worldwide. Lowering bad cholesterol, called LDL-C, is a key way to reduce the risk of heart attacks and strokes. Usually, strong cholesterol drugs are given only to people who already have heart disease.

**What happened** – Researchers studied a drug called evolocumab, which lowers LDL-C by about 60%. They gave it to 3,655 people with diabetes who were at high risk but did not have artery plaque yet. The patients took evolocumab injections every two weeks along with their usual cholesterol medicines. After nearly five years, those on evolocumab had a 31% lower chance of having their first heart attack, stroke, or dying from heart disease.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that using powerful cholesterol-lowering medicine earlier, before artery damage is visible, can prevent serious heart problems. It could change how doctors treat people with diabetes who are at risk but don’t yet have heart disease.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to test if evolocumab also helps other groups of high-risk patients without artery plaque. They will also watch for any long-term side effects to confirm the drug is safe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Giving evolocumab early to high-risk diabetes patients without artery plaque cuts their chance of first major heart problems by nearly one-third.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament contre le cholestérol réduit de 31 % le risque de première crise cardiaque chez les diabétiques à haut risque
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les maladies cardiaques sont la première cause de mortalité dans le monde. Réduire le mauvais cholestérol, appelé LDL-C, est une méthode clé pour diminuer le risque de crise cardiaque et d’accident vasculaire cérébral. Habituellement, les médicaments puissants contre le cholestérol sont réservés aux personnes ayant déjà une maladie cardiaque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié l’évolocumab, un médicament qui réduit le LDL-C d’environ 60 %. Ils l’ont donné à 3 655 personnes diabétiques à haut risque mais sans plaque dans les artères. Les patients ont reçu des injections toutes les deux semaines en plus de leurs traitements habituels. Après presque cinq ans, ceux qui prenaient l’évolocumab avaient 31 % moins de risque d’avoir une première crise cardiaque, un AVC ou de mourir d’une maladie cardiaque.

**Impact** – Cela montre qu’utiliser un traitement puissant contre le cholestérol plus tôt, avant que les artères soient endommagées, peut prévenir des problèmes cardiaques graves. Cela pourrait changer la façon dont les médecins traitent les diabétiques à risque.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs veulent vérifier si l’évolocumab aide aussi d’autres groupes à haut risque sans plaque artérielle. Ils surveilleront aussi les effets à long terme pour confirmer la sécurité du médicament.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’évolocumab donné tôt aux diabétiques à haut risque sans plaque réduit leur risque de première crise cardiaque ou AVC d’environ un tiers.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento para el colesterol reduce en un 31 % el riesgo de primer infarto en diabéticos de alto riesgo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enfermedades del corazón son la principal causa de muerte en el mundo. Bajar el colesterol malo, llamado LDL-C, es una forma clave de reducir el riesgo de infartos y derrames cerebrales. Normalmente, los medicamentos fuertes para el colesterol se usan solo en personas que ya tienen enfermedad cardíaca.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron un medicamento llamado evolocumab, que reduce el LDL-C en aproximadamente un 60 %. Lo dieron a 3,655 personas con diabetes de alto riesgo pero sin placa en las arterias. Los pacientes recibieron inyecciones cada dos semanas junto con sus medicamentos habituales. Después de casi cinco años, quienes tomaron evolocumab tuvieron un 31 % menos de riesgo de sufrir su primer infarto, derrame cerebral o muerte por enfermedad cardíaca.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que usar medicina fuerte para bajar el colesterol antes de que las arterias estén dañadas puede prevenir problemas graves del corazón. Podría cambiar cómo los doctores tratan a personas con diabetes que están en riesgo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren probar si evolocumab también ayuda a otros grupos de alto riesgo sin placa arterial. También vigilarán posibles efectos secundarios a largo plazo para asegurar que el medicamento sea seguro.

**Frase clave** – Dar evolocumab temprano a pacientes diabéticos de alto riesgo sin placa arterial reduce casi en un tercio el riesgo de problemas cardíacos graves por primera vez.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a strong, healthy heart shaped from smooth, interlocking golden arteries gently encased by protective, glowing shields symbolizing evolocumab’s effect, with a subtle network of delicate silver threads representing blood vessels in the background, all rendered in warm natural tones of soft reds, golds, and creams.

articles.title: Simple therapies beat drugs for knee arthritis pain relief
articles.summary: A major analysis of nearly 10,000 patients shows that simple, non-drug treatments like knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise can significantly ease knee osteoarthritis symptoms. These approaches not only reduce pain and improve mobility, but also avoid the risks tied to common medications. The findings suggest that low-cost, accessible therapies could play a bigger role in how doctors treat arthritis in the future.
articles.content:
A new large-scale analysis has identified knee braces, water therapy, and exercise as the most effective non-drug options for managing knee osteoarthritis. The findings come from a meta-analysis published in the open-access journal PLOS One.

Knee osteoarthritis (KOA) is a widespread and often disabling condition that affects millions of older adults. It leads to ongoing pain and stiffness in the knee joint, making everyday movement more difficult. Many patients rely on anti-inflammatory medications, but these drugs can carry risks, including gastrointestinal and cardiovascular side effects.

Large Study Compares 12 Non-Drug Therapies

To better understand which non-drug treatments work best, researchers analyzed data from 139 clinical trials involving nearly 10,000 participants. The study compared 12 different therapies, including laser therapy, electrical stimulation, knee braces, insoles, kinesiology tape, water-based therapy, exercise, and ultrasound.

By combining results across all of these studies using a network meta-analysis, the researchers were able to rank each treatment based on its effectiveness.

Knee Braces, Hydrotherapy, and Exercise Lead

Knee braces ranked highest overall, showing strong results in reducing pain, improving joint function, and easing stiffness. Hydrotherapy -- exercises or treatments performed in warm water -- was especially helpful for pain relief. Regular exercise also delivered consistent benefits, improving both pain levels and physical function.

Some advanced treatments, such as high-intensity laser therapy and shock wave therapy, provided moderate improvements. In contrast, ultrasound consistently ranked as the least effective option.

Study Limitations and Future Research

The researchers note that variations in study design, smaller sample sizes in some trials, and differences in how long treatments were used could affect how precise the rankings are. Even so, the overall findings suggest that physical therapy approaches offer meaningful benefits without the risks linked to anti-inflammatory medications.

Future research should explore how combining different therapies might improve outcomes further and whether these approaches are cost-effective in real-world care.

Safer Alternatives to Pain Medications

The authors add: "Knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise are the most effective non-drug therapies for knee osteoarthritis. They reduce pain and improve mobility without the gastrointestinal or cardiovascular risks linked to common pain medications. Patients and clinicians should prioritize these evidence-based options."

"Our analysis of nearly 10,000 patients reveals that simple, accessible therapies like knee bracing and water-based exercise outperform high-tech options like ultrasound. This could reshape clinical guidelines to focus on safer, lower-cost interventions."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a large-scale meta-analysis showing that simple, low-cost, non-drug therapies like knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise effectively relieve knee osteoarthritis pain and improve mobility. This has broad, meaningful implications for millions of patients by offering safer alternatives to medications with harmful side effects, potentially reshaping clinical treatment guidelines.
articles.personality_title: Simple therapies like knee braces and exercise ease arthritis pain better than drugs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Knee osteoarthritis is a common condition where the knee joint becomes painful and stiff, making it hard for many older adults to move comfortably. People often take anti-inflammatory drugs to help, but these medicines can cause side effects like stomach problems or heart issues.

**What happened**
Researchers studied 139 clinical trials with almost 10,000 patients to find out which non-drug treatments work best for knee osteoarthritis. They compared 12 different therapies, including knee braces, water therapy (hydrotherapy), exercise, laser therapy, and ultrasound. The study found that knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise were the most effective ways to reduce pain and improve knee movement.

**Impact**
This is important because these simple treatments not only help people feel better but also avoid the risks that come with many pain medicines. Knee braces helped with pain and stiffness, hydrotherapy eased pain especially well, and exercise improved both pain and how well the knee worked. This means more people could use safer, cheaper treatments instead of relying mostly on drugs.

**What's next step**
Scientists want to look into whether combining these therapies can help even more and if using them is affordable in everyday medical care. The results could change how doctors treat knee arthritis by focusing more on these easy-to-use, low-cost options.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Simple, non-drug treatments like knee braces, water therapy, and exercise can relieve knee arthritis pain and improve movement better than many drug-based options, offering safer and more affordable care.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des thérapies simples comme les genouillères et l’exercice soulagent mieux l’arthrose du genou que les médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
L’arthrose du genou est une maladie fréquente qui rend l’articulation douloureuse et raide, ce qui complique les mouvements chez de nombreuses personnes âgées. Souvent, on prend des médicaments anti-inflammatoires, mais ces traitements peuvent provoquer des effets secondaires comme des problèmes d’estomac ou cardiaques.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Des chercheurs ont étudié 139 essais cliniques avec près de 10 000 patients pour savoir quelles thérapies sans médicament fonctionnent le mieux contre l’arthrose du genou. Ils ont comparé 12 traitements, dont les genouillères, l’hydrothérapie (exercices dans l’eau), l’exercice physique, la thérapie au laser et l’échographie. Ils ont découvert que les genouillères, l’hydrothérapie et l’exercice étaient les plus efficaces pour réduire la douleur et améliorer la mobilité du genou.

**Impact**
C’est important car ces traitements simples aident non seulement à mieux se sentir, mais évitent aussi les risques liés aux médicaments. Les genouillères ont réduit douleur et raideur, l’hydrothérapie a soulagé la douleur particulièrement bien, et l’exercice a amélioré la douleur et la fonction du genou. Cela signifie que davantage de personnes peuvent utiliser des traitements sûrs et peu coûteux au lieu de se fier principalement aux médicaments.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs veulent étudier si combiner ces thérapies peut donner de meilleurs résultats et si elles sont abordables dans la pratique médicale courante. Ces résultats pourraient changer la façon dont les médecins traitent l’arthrose du genou, en privilégiant ces options simples et peu coûteuses.

**En une phrase**
Des traitements simples sans médicament comme les genouillères, l’hydrothérapie et l’exercice soulagent la douleur de l’arthrose du genou et améliorent la mobilité mieux que de nombreux médicaments, offrant des soins plus sûrs et abordables.
articles.personality_title_es: Terapias simples como rodilleras y ejercicio alivian mejor el dolor de la artritis de rodilla que los medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
La osteoartritis de rodilla es una enfermedad común que causa dolor y rigidez en la articulación, dificultando el movimiento en muchas personas mayores. A menudo, se usan medicamentos antiinflamatorios, pero estos pueden causar efectos secundarios como problemas estomacales o cardíacos.

**Qué pasó**
Investigadores analizaron 139 ensayos clínicos con casi 10,000 pacientes para descubrir qué tratamientos sin medicamentos funcionan mejor para la osteoartritis de rodilla. Compararon 12 terapias, incluyendo rodilleras, hidroterapia (ejercicios en agua), ejercicio, terapia láser y ultrasonido. Encontraron que las rodilleras, la hidroterapia y el ejercicio eran las más efectivas para reducir el dolor y mejorar el movimiento de la rodilla.

**Impacto**
Esto es importante porque estos tratamientos simples no solo ayudan a sentirse mejor, sino que también evitan los riesgos de los medicamentos. Las rodilleras ayudaron con el dolor y la rigidez, la hidroterapia alivió especialmente el dolor, y el ejercicio mejoró tanto el dolor como la función de la rodilla. Esto significa que más personas podrían usar tratamientos seguros y económicos en lugar de depender principalmente de medicamentos.

**Próximo paso**
Los científicos quieren investigar si combinar estas terapias puede ayudar aún más y si son rentables en la atención médica diaria. Los resultados podrían cambiar la forma en que los médicos tratan la artritis de rodilla, enfocándose más en estas opciones fáciles y de bajo costo.

**Resumen en una frase**
Tratamientos simples sin medicamentos como rodilleras, terapia en agua y ejercicio pueden aliviar el dolor de la artritis de rodilla y mejorar el movimiento mejor que muchos medicamentos, ofreciendo cuidados más seguros y accesibles.

articles.title: Clitoral nerves mapped for first time, thirty years after male counterpart
articles.summary: PRESS REVIEW – Monday, March 30: Papers discuss the use of artificial intelligence in war and the ethics behind it. Staying with AI, German actress Collien Fernandes says Germany is a haven for perpetrators, after she revealed her husband has been sharing deepfake porn of her. Next: a story about squatters living in Gangnam, the richest part of Seoul. Finally, scientists have mapped the nerves of the clitoris for the first time.
articles.content:
One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

Reading time 3 min

PRESS REVIEW – Monday, March 30: Papers discuss the use of artificial intelligence in war and the ethics behind it. Staying with AI, German actress Collien Fernandes says Germany is a haven for perpetrators, after she revealed her husband has been sharing deepfake porn of her. Next: a story about squatters living in Gangnam, the richest part of Seoul. Finally, scientists have mapped the nerves of the clitoris for the first time.

Papers are discussing the use of artificial intelligence in warfare. The title of French daily Libération's front page reads "AI, war in automatic mode". It's an analysis piece that says AI now plays a significant role in military operations, including in Gaza, Iran and Ukraine. It talks about the many different uses of AI, like US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth's "AI first" approach. What has changed with AI is that there's no need for data analysts to analyse information collected by satellites or video anymore – it's now AI's work, so "in practical terms, AI accelerates the kill chain".

Read moreStreamlining the kill chain: how AI is changing modern warfare

The article also talks about the cognitive gap between the person who launches the attack and the victim. A study by King's College London says that AI models appear to make decisions that lead to escalation much faster – they threaten to use nuclear weapons in nearly 95 percent of the simulated scenarios. Le Monde interviewed a philosopher who specialises in military ethics. She believes that "the use of lethal autonomous weapons controlled by AI goes against the principles of a just war". She says that in order to ensure the proportionality of the strike, data needs to be verified in advance by humans, in order to carry out what's been dubbed "effective human oversight".

In some shocking news, German actress Collien Fernandes revealed publicly last week that her ex-partner had been spreading deepfake pornography of her – a case dubbed "digital rape". Der Spiegel reports that she said Germany is a haven for perpetrators and only 2.4 percent of digital violence is reported. She says that her own experience with law enforcement has been discouraging.

In other AI news, it turns out it's not a good idea to ask AI bots for personal advice. Twelve percent of US teenagers turn to chatbots for emotional support advice – they ask for relationship advice and even draft breakup texts. The problem, however, is that AI usually validates user behaviour and doesn't provide the "tough love" that's sometimes needed. AI just convinces people they are right and makes them less likely to apologise.

Moving to South Korea, The New York Times reports about "the squatters living in the richest part of Seoul": the Gangnam district. The feature article follows the story of Miss Lee. The government has offered more than $3,000 for her hut and told her to vacate or face eviction. But owning a home is a struggle and she has "nowhere else to go," so she is fighting to stay. In Gangnam, which inspired the K-pop hit "Gangnam Style", prices have been spiking faster and higher than anywhere else in Seoul.

Finally, there's some good news for women around the world: The Guardian reports that scientists have finally mapped the nerves inside the clitoris. This comes almost 30 years after they did the same thing for the penis. This is beneficial and not only for orgasms – understanding this organ could help prevent poorer sexual function after pelvic operations. It could also help with reconstructive surgery after female genital mutilation – "one of the most extreme examples of cultural misogyny". The Guardian writes that a cultural taboo around female sexuality has held back scientific investigations. The clitoris only entered standard medicine textbooks in the 20th century.

You can catch our press review every morning on France 24 at 7:20am and 9:20am (Paris time), from Monday to Friday.
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough: the first mapping of clitoral nerves, which has broad implications for women's health, sexual function, and reconstructive surgery after female genital mutilation. This discovery addresses a long-standing scientific gap due to cultural taboos and has meaningful potential benefits for many women worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map clitoral nerves for the first time, opening new health possibilities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For nearly 30 years, scientists had mapped the nerves of the penis but had not done the same for the clitoris. This gap was partly due to cultural taboos around female sexuality and a lack of medical study in this area.

**What happened** – Recently, researchers successfully mapped the nerves inside the clitoris for the first time. This important scientific work reveals the detailed nerve structure of this organ, which had been overlooked until now.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it can help doctors better understand female sexual health. It may improve treatments for women who experience poor sexual function, especially after pelvic surgeries. It also offers hope for better reconstructive surgery for women affected by female genital mutilation, a harmful practice that damages this sensitive area.

**What's next step** – Scientists and doctors will use this new knowledge to improve medical care and surgical techniques. It may lead to better education about female anatomy and help break down cultural taboos that have slowed research in the past.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Mapping the clitoral nerves for the first time fills a long-standing gap in medical knowledge and can improve health and surgery options for women worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cartographient pour la première fois les nerfs du clitoris, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités de santé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant près de 30 ans, les scientifiques ont cartographié les nerfs du pénis mais pas ceux du clitoris. Ce retard est dû en partie aux tabous culturels autour de la sexualité féminine et au manque d’études médicales dans ce domaine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, des chercheurs ont réussi à cartographier pour la première fois les nerfs à l’intérieur du clitoris. Ce travail scientifique important révèle la structure nerveuse détaillée de cet organe, longtemps négligé.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle peut aider les médecins à mieux comprendre la santé sexuelle des femmes. Elle pourrait améliorer les traitements pour celles qui ont des problèmes sexuels, surtout après des opérations pelviennes. Elle offre aussi de l’espoir pour de meilleures chirurgies reconstructives chez les femmes victimes de mutilations génitales féminines, une pratique nuisible qui endommage cette zone sensible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques et médecins utiliseront ces nouvelles connaissances pour améliorer les soins et les techniques chirurgicales. Cela pourrait aussi aider à mieux éduquer sur l’anatomie féminine et à lever les tabous culturels qui ont freiné la recherche.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La cartographie des nerfs du clitoris comble une lacune médicale ancienne et peut améliorer la santé et la chirurgie des femmes dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean por primera vez los nervios del clítoris, abriendo nuevas posibilidades de salud
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante casi 30 años, los científicos habían mapeado los nervios del pene, pero no lo habían hecho con los del clítoris. Esto se debió en parte a tabúes culturales sobre la sexualidad femenina y la falta de estudios médicos en este tema.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, investigadores lograron mapear por primera vez los nervios dentro del clítoris. Este importante trabajo científico muestra la estructura nerviosa detallada de este órgano, que había sido ignorado.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque puede ayudar a los médicos a comprender mejor la salud sexual femenina. Puede mejorar tratamientos para mujeres con problemas sexuales, especialmente después de cirugías pélvicas. También ofrece esperanza para mejores cirugías reconstructivas en mujeres afectadas por la mutilación genital femenina, una práctica dañina que afecta esta zona sensible.

**Próximo paso** – Científicos y médicos usarán este nuevo conocimiento para mejorar cuidados médicos y técnicas quirúrgicas. Esto también puede ayudar a educar mejor sobre la anatomía femenina y romper tabúes culturales que frenaron la investigación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Mapear los nervios del clítoris por primera vez llena un vacío antiguo en el conocimiento médico y puede mejorar la salud y las opciones quirúrgicas para mujeres en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, intricately detailed botanical illustration of a blossoming flower with a network of fine, glowing golden threads spreading outward from its center, symbolizing newly mapped nerves, set against a soft, warm background of muted earth tones and gentle light.
